facts.text: The Royal Game of Ur, discovered in a tomb in modern-day Iraq, dates back to approximately 2600 BCE and is one of the oldest known board games.
Monopoly is the best-selling board game in history, with over 275 million copies sold worldwide.
A standard Rubik's Cube has 43 quintillion possible configurations but can always be solved in 20 moves or fewer.
Dungeons & Dragons was first published in 1974 by Gary Gygax and Dave Arneson, creating the tabletop role-playing game genre.
The word 'Scrabble' means to scratch or grope around frantically, which is fitting for a game about finding words from random letters.
Gen Con, one of the largest tabletop gaming conventions in the world, was founded by Gary Gygax in 1968 in Lake Geneva, Wisconsin.
Settlers of Catan, designed by Klaus Teuber, has sold over 32 million copies and is credited with launching the modern board game renaissance.
The game of Go originated in China more than 2,500 years ago and is considered the oldest board game still played in its original form.
Jigsaw puzzles were originally created around 1760 by John Spilsbury, a London mapmaker who mounted a map on wood and cut it into pieces.
The Spiel des Jahres (Game of the Year) is a prestigious German award established in 1978 that has become the most influential board game award in the world.
Magic: The Gathering, created by Richard Garfield and released in 1993, was the first collectible card game and now has over 35 million players.
Backgammon is one of the oldest known board games, with origins dating back roughly 5,000 years to Mesopotamia.
The original Monopoly game was based on 'The Landlord's Game,' patented by Elizabeth Magie in 1904 to demonstrate the negative aspects of land monopolies.
BoardGameGeek, the most popular online database for board games, was founded in 2000 and now catalogs over 130,000 games.
Pandemic, designed by Matt Leacock and released in 2008, is one of the most popular cooperative board games ever made.
The crossword puzzle was invented by journalist Arthur Wynne and first published in the New York World newspaper on December 21, 1913.
Ernő Rubik, a Hungarian professor of architecture, invented the Rubik's Cube in 1974 as a teaching tool to help students understand 3D geometry.
Risk was invented by French filmmaker Albert Lamorisse and originally called 'La Conquête du Monde' (The Conquest of the World).
Ticket to Ride, designed by Alan R. Moon, won the Spiel des Jahres in 2004 and has sold over 10 million copies worldwide.
The highest single-word Scrabble score ever recorded in competition is 392 points for the word 'CAZIQUES' (plural of a type of bird).
Clue (known as Cluedo outside North America) was invented by Anthony Pratt, a British musician, during World War II air-raid blackouts.
Critical Role, a web series where voice actors play Dungeons & Dragons, helped popularize tabletop RPGs and raised over $11 million on Kickstarter for an animated series.
Senet is an ancient Egyptian board game dating back to around 3100 BCE, and game boards have been found in the tombs of pharaohs.
The first escape room opened in Kyoto, Japan, in 2007, created by Takao Kato of the publishing company SCRAP.
Azul, designed by Michael Kiesling and inspired by Portuguese azulejo tiles, won the Spiel des Jahres in 2018.
Uno was created in 1971 by Merle Robbins, a barber from Cincinnati, Ohio, who initially sold copies from his barbershop.
The Pokémon Trading Card Game was first published in Japan in October 1996 by Media Factory and later in North America by Wizards of the Coast.
Gloomhaven, designed by Isaac Childres, was the highest-rated board game on BoardGameGeek for several years and raised over $4.4 million on Kickstarter.
A standard set of polyhedral dice used in tabletop RPGs includes d4, d6, d8, d10, d12, and d20 — the d20 being the most iconic.
Mancala is a family of board games played around the world, with evidence of play dating back to the 6th or 7th century in East Africa.
The Game of Life was originally created by Milton Bradley in 1860 as 'The Checkered Game of Life,' making it one of the first board games mass-produced in the United States.
Wingspan, designed by Elizabeth Hargrave and published by Stonemaier Games, won the 2019 Kennerspiel des Jahres and features over 170 unique bird cards.
Cards Against Humanity started as a Kickstarter project in 2011 and became one of the best-selling party games without any traditional marketing.
Essen Spiel, held annually in Essen, Germany, is the world's largest board game fair, attracting over 200,000 visitors each year.
The world record for solving a Rubik's Cube is under 3.5 seconds, set in official World Cube Association competition.
Reiner Knizia, one of the most prolific board game designers, has published over 700 games, including Tigris & Euphrates, Lost Cities, and Ra.
Trivial Pursuit was invented in 1979 by Canadian journalists Scott Abbott and Chris Haney after they realized some pieces of their Scrabble set were missing.
Stratego, based on the earlier French game L'Attaque, involves hidden pieces and bluffing, making it a classic military strategy game.
Sudoku, despite its Japanese name meaning 'single number,' was actually popularized by an American named Howard Garns who created it in 1979.
The first World Chess Championship was held in 1886 between Wilhelm Steinitz and Johannes Zukertort.
Terraforming Mars, designed by Jacob Fryxelius, challenges players to make Mars habitable and has won numerous awards since its 2016 release.
Parker Brothers initially rejected Monopoly, citing '52 fundamental playing errors,' before eventually purchasing the rights from Charles Darrow in 1935.
The modern jigsaw puzzle industry produces over 10 million puzzles per year in the United States alone.
Exploding Kittens became the most-backed Kickstarter project in history at the time of its campaign in 2015, raising $8.8 million.
Spirit Island, designed by R. Eric Reuss, is a cooperative strategy game where players take the role of island spirits defending against colonizers.
The longest game of Monopoly ever played lasted 70 straight days.
Vlaada Chvátil, a Czech game designer, is known for creating diverse games including Codenames, Through the Ages, and Galaxy Trucker.
Bridge is one of the most widely played card games in the world, with an estimated 200 million players across all countries.
Klaus Teuber, creator of Settlers of Catan, was a dental technician before becoming a full-time game designer.
The first Gen Con convention in 1968 attracted only about 100 attendees; today it draws over 70,000 people annually.
Yahtzee was originally called 'Yatzie' and was invented by a Canadian couple who played it on their yacht.
Sorry! is based on the ancient Indian game Pachisi, which dates back to the 6th century and was played on a cloth board.
The most expensive Monopoly set ever made was worth $2 million, featuring gold and jewel-encrusted pieces created by jeweler Sidney Mobell.
Pathfinder, published by Paizo, was created as a continuation of D&D's 3.5 edition rules when Wizards of the Coast moved on to 4th edition.
Codenames, designed by Vlaada Chvátil, won the Spiel des Jahres in 2016 and became a massive hit in both casual and competitive gaming circles.
The term 'FLGS' stands for Friendly Local Game Store, a cornerstone of the board gaming community where people gather to play and buy games.
PAX (Penny Arcade Expo) is a series of gaming conventions that cover both video games and tabletop gaming, founded by the creators of the Penny Arcade webcomic.
Chess, despite being one of the most popular board games, is believed to have originated in India around the 6th century CE as 'chaturanga.'
The largest jigsaw puzzle commercially produced contained over 40,000 pieces and measured about 22 feet by 6 feet when completed.
Carcassonne, named after the medieval fortress city in France, was designed by Klaus-Jürgen Wrede and won the Spiel des Jahres in 2001.
The 'Legacy' game concept, where the board and rules permanently change between sessions, was pioneered by Risk Legacy, designed by Rob Daviau in 2011.
In Scrabble, the blank tile is worth zero points but is often considered the most valuable tile due to its versatility.
The oldest known dice were found in a 5,000-year-old backgammon set in southeastern Iran.
Battleship was originally a pencil-and-paper game played during World War I before Milton Bradley turned it into a plastic board game in 1967.
Kickstarter has become a major platform for board game publishing, with the tabletop games category consistently being one of its highest-funded categories.
The first official Scrabble tournament was held in 1973, and the game has since developed a highly competitive international scene.
Game cafés, where customers pay to play from a library of board games, have become increasingly popular worldwide since the 2010s.
Dungeons & Dragons uses a d20 (20-sided die) as its primary mechanic, giving it the nickname 'the d20 system.'
The Game of the Goose, dating back to 15th-century Florence, is considered the prototype for many modern roll-and-move board games.
Pandemic Legacy: Season 1, designed by Rob Daviau and Matt Leacock, is often rated as one of the greatest board games of all time on BoardGameGeek.
Hasbro, one of the world's largest toy and game companies, owns the rights to Monopoly, Scrabble (in NA), Risk, Clue, and many other classic games.
The Rubik's Cube became a global sensation in the 1980s, selling over 350 million units, making it the world's best-selling puzzle.
Victorian parlor games like charades, blindman's buff, and card games were major forms of entertainment before the rise of commercial board games.
7 Wonders, designed by Antoine Bauza, can be played with up to 7 players and uses a card-drafting mechanism that allows simultaneous play.
The word 'checkmate' in chess comes from the Persian phrase 'shāh māt,' meaning 'the king is dead.'
Monopoly was first sold by Parker Brothers on February 6, 1935, and became the best-selling game in America that year.
The average American household owns about 12 board games.
Dominion, designed by Donald X. Vaccarino, popularized the deck-building genre when it won the Spiel des Jahres in 2009.
Rubik's Cube competitions are organized by the World Cube Association, which recognizes events like one-handed solving and blindfolded solving.
The Chinese game of Mahjong dates back to the Qing dynasty and involves skill, strategy, and a degree of luck with 144 tiles.
Scrabble was invented by architect Alfred Mosher Butts during the Great Depression and was originally called 'Lexiko' and then 'Criss-Crosswords.'
Board game sales surged during the COVID-19 pandemic, with some companies reporting increases of 20-50% in revenue.
The Settlers of Catan was first published in Germany in 1995 and has been translated into over 40 languages.
In competitive Scrabble, players memorize the Official Scrabble Players Dictionary, which contains over 190,000 legal words.
Gary Gygax's original Dungeons & Dragons game was largely inspired by J.R.R. Tolkien's The Lord of the Rings and miniature wargaming.
Dixit, a storytelling card game with beautifully illustrated cards, won the Spiel des Jahres in 2010.
The first crossword puzzle book was published by Simon & Schuster in 1924, and it included a pencil with each copy.
Catan has been adopted as a teaching tool in some schools to help students learn about resource management and negotiation.
The Rubik's Cube was originally called the 'Magic Cube' (Bűvös Kocka) when Ernő Rubik invented it in Hungary in 1974.
Twilight Imperium, published by Fantasy Flight Games, is famous for its epic scope, with games often lasting 6 to 12 hours.
The most expensive board game ever sold at auction was a rare 1933 hand-drawn Monopoly board by Charles Darrow, which sold for $146,500.
Cooperative board games, where all players work together against the game, have become increasingly popular since the release of Pandemic in 2008.
Netflix adapted the board game Settlers of Catan into a film project, reflecting growing mainstream interest in tabletop gaming.
The word 'quiz' was supposedly invented in 1791 by a Dublin theater owner who bet he could introduce a meaningless word into the language overnight.
Ticket to Ride was first published in 2004 and has spawned numerous expansions covering maps of Europe, Asia, Africa, and more.
The first commercial jigsaw puzzles were actually called 'dissected maps' and were used as educational tools to teach geography.
Root, designed by Cole Wehrle, is an asymmetric war game set in a woodland where each faction plays by completely different rules.
In the game of Go, there are more possible board positions than there are atoms in the observable universe.
Scrabble has different letter distributions and point values depending on the language edition — the French version includes letters like É and Ç.
The D&D cartoon series aired from 1983 to 1985 and followed a group of children transported into the realm of Dungeons & Dragons.
Wits & Wagers, designed by Dominic Crapuchettes, combines trivia with betting and is designed so that even players who don't know answers can win.
The first World Puzzle Championship was held in New York City in 1992.
Betrayal at House on the Hill features 50 possible horror scenarios, meaning each playthrough can be a completely different experience.
Elizabeth Hargrave, designer of Wingspan, was the first woman to win the Kennerspiel des Jahres award.
The oldest known checkers-like game pieces were found in the ancient city of Ur, dating to approximately 3000 BCE.
Cranium, which combines trivia, word games, drawing, and acting, was the first game to be sold exclusively at Starbucks before reaching traditional retailers.
Kingdomino, designed by Bruno Cathala, won the Spiel des Jahres in 2017 and is based on the classic domino-matching concept applied to kingdom building.
The international Scrabble world championship, known as the World Scrabble Championship, has been held since 1991.
Pictionary was created by Rob Angel in 1985 and became a party game sensation, selling millions of copies worldwide.
The term 'Eurogame' or 'German-style board game' refers to games that emphasize strategy over luck and avoid player elimination.
Clue was first manufactured by Waddingtons in Leeds, England, in 1949 before Parker Brothers brought it to the United States.
The most landed-on space in Monopoly is Jail, followed by Illinois Avenue and Go.
Agricola, designed by Uwe Rosenberg, is a farming-themed strategy game that was the top-rated game on BoardGameGeek before Gloomhaven.
A 'meeple' is a small wooden person-shaped playing piece, first used in Carcassonne, and the word has been adopted broadly in the board gaming community.
The game of Othello (also known as Reversi) takes 'a minute to learn, a lifetime to master,' according to its famous marketing slogan.
King of Tokyo, designed by Richard Garfield (also the creator of Magic: The Gathering), is a dice-rolling game where players are monsters attacking Tokyo.
The first modern escape room game for the home was 'Exit: The Game' series by Kosmos, which won the Kennerspiel des Jahres in 2017.
Connect Four was first sold by Milton Bradley in 1974 and is a solved game — the first player can always win with perfect play.
The Rubik's Cube has inspired many variations, including the 2x2 (Pocket Cube), 4x4 (Rubik's Revenge), and cubes up to 21x21.
In 2017, crowdfunding platform Kickstarter reported that tabletop games had raised over $1 billion on the platform since its founding.
Trivial Pursuit was first released in Canada in 1981 before becoming an international phenomenon.
Catapult-based games like Angry Birds and dexterity games trace their roots to old folk games involving flicking and tossing objects at targets.
Power Grid, designed by Friedemann Friese, is a resource management game where players compete to supply electricity to cities.
The phrase 'back to square one' is thought to originate from board games where players must return to the starting square.
Dead of Winter is a semi-cooperative survival game where players must work together while dealing with hidden traitor mechanics.
Small World, designed by Philippe Keyaerts, involves fantasy races vying for control of a world that is literally too small for everyone.
The largest game of Monopoly was played on a board measuring 9,689 square feet, set up in a park in the Netherlands.
Scattergories, released by Milton Bradley in 1988, challenges players to think of words in categories that begin with a specific letter before time runs out.
Terra Mystica is a complex strategy game that has inspired several spiritual successors, including Gaia Project, which moves the theme to space.
The first national Monopoly Championship in the US was held in 1973.
Puerto Rico, designed by Andreas Seyfarth, was one of the highest-rated strategy games on BoardGameGeek for many years.
Hanabi, a cooperative card game where players can see everyone's cards except their own, won the Spiel des Jahres in 2013.
The oldest known example of a chess piece dates to around 760 CE and was found in Uzbekistan.
Forbidden Island, designed by Matt Leacock, is a cooperative game where players work together to recover treasures from a sinking island.
Apples to Apples, released in 1999, helped pave the way for modern party card games like Cards Against Humanity.
The traditional Indian game Snakes and Ladders was originally called Moksha Patam and was designed to teach moral virtues and vices.
Blokus, a territorial strategy game using tetromino-shaped pieces, was created by French mathematician Bernard Tavitian in 2000.
Mysterium is a cooperative deduction game where one player acts as a ghost communicating through abstract vision cards to help other players solve a murder.
The original printing of Dungeons & Dragons consisted of only 1,000 copies, which sold out in about 11 months.
In Japan, board gaming (particularly Go and Shogi) has professional leagues with players who train from childhood.
Sagrada, a dice-drafting game, challenges players to build stained-glass windows for the Sagrada Família cathedral in Barcelona.
The Q tile in Scrabble is worth 10 points, and QI is the most commonly played Q word in competitive Scrabble.
Splendor, a gem-trading game designed by Marc André, was nominated for the Spiel des Jahres in 2014 and has sold millions of copies.
The first International Puzzle Day was celebrated on January 29, 1995.
Mousetrap was one of the first mass-produced three-dimensional board games when it was released in 1963.
Everdell combines worker placement and tableau building with a charming woodland theme, and its detailed 3D Ever Tree component sets it apart visually.
The original Trivial Pursuit game contained 6,000 questions across six categories.
In Monopoly, players who stay in Jail later in the game actually have a strategic advantage, as they collect rent without risking landing on developed properties.
Viticulture, designed by Jamey Stegmaier, is a worker-placement game about running a vineyard in Tuscany.
The game of Nim is a mathematical strategy game that was one of the first games solved by computer analysis.
Photosynthesis, by Blue Orange Games, has players grow trees in a forest, competing for sunlight as the sun moves around the board.
The German games market is the largest in Europe, and Germany has the highest per-capita spending on board games.
Boggle, invented by Allan Turoff and first distributed by Parker Brothers in 1972, challenges players to find words in a 4x4 grid of lettered dice.
Pandemic Legacy: Season 1 was released in October 2015 and immediately became one of the highest-rated games ever.
Tabletop, a web series hosted by Wil Wheaton from 2012 to 2019, showcased board games and significantly boosted awareness of modern tabletop gaming.
The '1000 Blank White Cards' is an unusual card game where players create their own cards with unique rules during play.
Star Realms is a deck-building card game designed by Magic: The Gathering Pro Tour champions Rob Dougherty and Darwin Kastle.
The game Qwirkle, involving matching tiles by color or shape, won the Spiel des Jahres in 2011.
In 1991, Hasbro acquired Parker Brothers and Milton Bradley, consolidating many classic board games under a single corporate umbrella.
Catan Studios reported selling over 1 million copies of Settlers of Catan in the US in a single year.
Galaxy Trucker, designed by Vlaada Chvátil, has players frantically build spaceships from junk and then watch them fall apart during a space voyage.
Richard Garfield designed Magic: The Gathering while he was a graduate student in combinatorial mathematics at the University of Pennsylvania.
The party game Telestrations combines drawing and the telephone game, creating hilariously garbled interpretations of original words.
Shadows over Camelot was one of the first modern board games to feature a hidden traitor mechanic.
The Rubik's Cube World Championship has been held since 1982, with the first competition taking place in Budapest, Hungary.
In Scrabble, the highest possible opening move scores 128 points for the word 'MUZJIKS,' a variant spelling of Russian peasants.
Brass: Birmingham, designed by Martin Wallace, is consistently ranked among the top strategy games on BoardGameGeek.
The first commercially produced board game in the United States was 'The Mansion of Happiness,' published in 1843.
Takenoko, designed by Antoine Bauza, features an adorable panda figure that players move around a bamboo garden.
Skull (originally Skull & Roses) is a bluffing game that uses only coasters-like cards, yet is considered one of the best pure bluffing games.
The average hobby board game takes about 18 months to design, playtest, and produce before reaching consumers.
Blood Rage, designed by Eric Lang, combines Viking mythology with area-control strategy and drafting mechanics.
Dungeons & Dragons Fifth Edition was released in 2014 and became the most commercially successful edition in the game's history.
The traditional game Nine Men's Morris has been found carved into the roofing slabs of an ancient Egyptian temple at Kurna, dating to around 1400 BCE.
Catan Junior, Ticket to Ride: First Journey, and My First Carcassonne are examples of gateway games designed for younger players.
The fastest Rubik's Cube solve by a robot is under 0.5 seconds, achieved by machines using specialized gripping mechanisms.
Century: Spice Road is a card-driven engine-building game that draws comparisons to Splendor for its elegant simplicity.
Patchwork, designed by Uwe Rosenberg, is a two-player game about quilting that is praised for its tight strategic depth.
The first mass-market version of The Game of Life was released in 1960, exactly 100 years after Milton Bradley's original version.
Sheriff of Nottingham is a bluffing and negotiation game where players try to smuggle contraband goods past a rotating sheriff.
In chess, there are 318,979,564,000 ways to play the first four moves.
The 'German invasion' of board games in the late 1990s and 2000s brought European-style strategy games to North American audiences.
Love Letter, designed by Seiji Kanai, is a minimalist card game with only 16 cards that became a worldwide hit.
Scythe, designed by Jamey Stegmaier, is set in an alternate 1920s Europe with mechs and farming, and became one of the most successful Kickstarter board games.
The Parker Brothers company was founded by George S. Parker in 1883, when he was just 16 years old.
The dice tower, a device for rolling dice fairly, has been used since the time of the Roman Empire.
Tigris & Euphrates, designed by Reiner Knizia, is set in ancient Mesopotamia and is considered one of the greatest strategy board games ever made.
Cards Against Humanity famously sold boxes of 'nothing' and actual bull feces as holiday promotions, both of which sold out.
The World Boardgaming Championships have been held annually since 1999 in Seven Springs, Pennsylvania.
Great Western Trail, designed by Alexander Pfister, challenges players to herd cattle along a trail from Texas to Kansas City.
The game Operation was invented by John Spinello, a University of Illinois student, who sold the concept for just $500.
Isle of Cats is a card-drafting and polyomino-placement game about rescuing cats from an island before the arrival of an evil lord.
The oldest known board game piece is a painted stone from the game of 58 Holes, found in present-day Azerbaijan and dating to about 2000 BCE.
Camel Up is a racing and betting game where unpredictable camel stacking creates hilarious moments, and it won the Spiel des Jahres in 2014.
Magic: The Gathering has printed over 20,000 unique cards since its inception in 1993.
Decrypto, a word-deduction team game, is often compared to Codenames but adds a layer of complexity with encrypted communication.
The first International Table Top Day was celebrated on March 30, 2013, founded by Geek & Sundry.
The 'worker placement' mechanic, a staple of modern board games, was popularized by the game Caylus, designed by William Attia in 2005.
The Chinese board game Xiangqi (Chinese Chess) has been played for over a thousand years and has subtle but significant differences from Western chess.
Sleeping Gods, designed by Ryan Laukat, is a storybook game where players explore a strange world across a massive, multi-session campaign.
In competitive Magic: The Gathering, a single card called Black Lotus from the original 1993 set has sold for over $500,000.
The game Candy Land was created in 1949 by Eleanor Abbott while she was recovering from polio in a hospital ward.
Quacks of Quedlinburg, a push-your-luck bag-building game, won the Kennerspiel des Jahres in 2018.
The concept of 'legacy' board games, where choices permanently alter the game, was inspired by Rob Daviau's frustration with games resetting every time.
Clank!, designed by Paul Dennen, combines deck-building with dungeon exploration and a push-your-luck dragon mechanic.
The first Dungeons & Dragons Basic Set was released in 1977 to make the game more accessible to new players.
Sushi Go!, designed by Phil Walker-Harding, is a fast-playing card-drafting game themed around a conveyor belt sushi restaurant.
The board game Mousetrap took over 10 years to develop due to engineering challenges with its elaborate Rube Goldberg machine.
Arkham Horror, published by Fantasy Flight Games, is a cooperative game set in H.P. Lovecraft's Cthulhu Mythos universe.
Ravensburger, the German company famous for jigsaw puzzles, was founded in 1883 and now produces over 8,000 different products.
Champions of Midgard combines Viking-themed worker placement with dice-based combat against mythological creatures.
The first documented reference to playing cards dates back to 9th-century China during the Tang dynasty.
Concordia, designed by Mac Gerdts, is a peaceful strategy game about trading and colonizing in the Roman Empire.
Jenga was created by Leslie Scott based on a game that her family played with wooden building blocks in Ghana in the 1970s.
The term 'analysis paralysis' is commonly used in board gaming to describe players who take excessively long turns overthinking decisions.
Roll for the Galaxy uses custom dice to represent workers in a space-themed civilization-building game.
Monopoly has been published in over 114 countries and in more than 47 languages, including Braille editions.
Charterstone, designed by Jamey Stegmaier, is a legacy worker-placement game where players build a village over 12 game sessions.
The World Scrabble Champion from 2015 was Nigel Richards, who famously also won the French Scrabble Championship despite not speaking French.
The standard Monopoly board features 40 spaces, including 28 properties, 3 Chance spaces, 3 Community Chest spaces, and various special squares.
Cascadia, a tile-laying and token-drafting game about the Pacific Northwest, won the Spiel des Jahres in 2022.
The 'roll and write' game genre, including games like Yahtzee and newer titles like Welcome To..., has seen a major resurgence in recent years.
In 1935, the unemployment rate in the United States was around 20%, contributing to the massive popularity of the escapist game Monopoly.
Mechs vs. Minions, based on League of Legends, was produced by Riot Games and praised for its exceptional production quality.
The Chinese game Wei Qi (known as Go in Japan) uses a 19×19 grid and is considered one of the four essential arts of the Chinese scholar.
The Spiel des Jahres jury considers accessibility and fun over complexity, which is why many winners are family-weight games.
Captain Sonar is a real-time team game where two submarine crews hunt each other simultaneously, creating intense competitive chaos.
Milton Bradley, the man, was originally a lithographer who printed Abraham Lincoln's portrait before turning to game manufacturing.
Jaipur is a two-player trading card game set in Rajasthan, India, where players compete to become the Maharaja's personal trader.
Trivial Pursuit's creators Chris Haney and Scott Abbott reportedly spent $75 on a prototype before the game became a multimillion-dollar franchise.
Risk was first published in France in 1957 as 'La Conquête du Monde' before Parker Brothers released it in the United States in 1959.
Just One, a cooperative word-guessing party game, won the Spiel des Jahres in 2019.
The concept of a 'Dungeon Master' or 'Game Master' running a tabletop RPG session was revolutionary when D&D introduced it.
Mansions of Madness combines a board game with an app, using digital technology to manage complex scenarios in a Lovecraftian setting.
The Parker Brothers rejected the game of Monopoly initially, listing 52 reasons why it would not sell.
Santorini is a strategy game based on building towers on the Greek island, with a simple rule set that belies deep strategic possibilities.
Above and Below, designed by Ryan Laukat, combines village-building with a storybook mechanic for exploring a subterranean world.
There are exactly 12 unique pentominoes (five-square polyomino shapes), and they have been used in puzzles since at least the 1900s.
The board game cafe trend started in South Korea and has since spread to major cities across Europe, North America, and beyond.
Tak is a two-player strategy game featured in Patrick Rothfuss's fantasy novel 'The Wise Man's Fear,' later produced as a real game by Cheapass Games.
Betrayal at Baldur's Gate adapts the Betrayal at House on the Hill formula to a Dungeons & Dragons setting.
The first commercial Sudoku puzzles were published by Dell Magazines in the United States in 1979 under the name 'Number Place.'
Inis is a strategy game about Celtic mythology where players compete to become the High King of Ireland using card drafting and area control.
The World Chess Championship between Bobby Fischer and Boris Spassky in 1972 was dubbed 'The Match of the Century.'
Welcome To... is a 'flip and write' game where players design 1950s American neighborhoods, and nearly any number of people can play simultaneously.
Forbidden Desert, a sequel to Forbidden Island, has players cooperatively searching for a legendary flying machine buried beneath the sand.
The average Monopoly game lasts between 60 and 90 minutes, though house rules can extend games for hours.
Unmatched is a fighting game system that pits characters from various genres (like Bigfoot, Bruce Lee, and Medusa) against each other.
An AI program called AlphaGo defeated the world champion Go player Lee Sedol in 2016, a landmark moment in artificial intelligence.
The 15-puzzle, a sliding tile game invented in the 1870s, created a global craze comparable to the later Rubik's Cube mania.
Wavelength is a social guessing game where players try to read each other's minds by placing a dial on a spectrum between two opposing concepts.
Some ancient Roman board games were carved into the stone steps of the Colosseum, where spectators played while waiting for events.
Rising Sun, designed by Eric Lang, brings Japanese mythology to life with a beautiful miniatures-heavy strategy game.
The world record for the most people playing a single board game simultaneously is over 1,000 players in a single Settlers of Catan event.
Through the Ages: A New Story of Civilization, designed by Vlaada Chvátil, is a card-based civilization-building game spanning thousands of years of history.
Spiel des Jahres winners typically see sales increases of 300-500% after winning the award.
Isle of Skye, a tile-placement game set in Scotland, won the Kennerspiel des Jahres in 2016.
The concept of 'table presence' describes how visually impressive a board game looks when set up, which has become increasingly important in game design.
The game Machi Koro, designed by Masao Suganuma, is a Japanese city-building card game that uses dice and engine-building mechanics.
Crokinole, a dexterity-based disc-flicking game, has been played in Canada since the 1870s and has seen a renaissance in modern gaming.
Unfathomable, formerly known as Battlestar Galactica: The Board Game, features hidden loyalties and social deduction on a ship at sea.
The Game of Life was updated in 2007 to include contemporary career options and a path to skip college.
Descent: Legends of the Dark combines a board game with a companion app to create a fully cooperative dungeon-crawling experience.
The original Dungeons & Dragons Monster Manual, published in 1977, introduced iconic creatures like the Beholder and the Mind Flayer.
Stone Age, a worker-placement game set in prehistoric times, introduced many players to the genre with its accessible gameplay.
The Monopoly Speed Die, introduced in 2007, was designed to speed up gameplay by adding a third die with special actions.
Pandemic Legacy: Season 2 takes place 71 years after a global plague and starts with a nearly blank game board that players explore over time.
Onitama is a two-player abstract strategy game inspired by martial arts, using only a 5x5 grid and five movement cards.
The Great Dalmuti, designed by Richard Garfield, is a card game about social hierarchy that physically seats players based on their rank each round.
Board game insert organizers and custom 3D-printed components have become a thriving cottage industry within the hobby.
The most funded tabletop game on Kickstarter is Frosthaven, a sequel to Gloomhaven, which raised over $12.9 million in 2020.
War, the simple card game, requires zero decision-making and is entirely determined by the initial shuffle of the deck.
Cosmic Encounter, first published in 1977, features over 50 alien races with unique powers and is considered a pioneer of asymmetric game design.
The Sherlock Holmes Consulting Detective series, first published in 1981, is one of the earliest examples of a narrative deduction game.
Calico, a tile-laying puzzle game about quilting and cats, challenges players to create specific patterns while attracting different cat breeds.
The original D&D game came in a small white box and included three booklets: Men & Magic, Monsters & Treasure, and The Underworld & Wilderness Adventures.
Quoridor is a two-player or four-player abstract strategy game where players try to reach the opposite side of the board while placing walls to block opponents.
The No Game No Life anime series is based around a fantasy world where all conflicts are resolved through games.
Coup is a bluffing and deduction card game where players claim roles they may or may not have and try to eliminate other players from the game.
The Rubik's Cube saw a resurgence in popularity in the 2000s thanks to online communities and 'speedcubing' competitions.
Survive: Escape from Atlantis!, originally released in 1982, has players fleeing a sinking island while sea monsters and sharks devour their opponents' pieces.
The board game industry was valued at over $13 billion globally by the early 2020s, with steady year-over-year growth.
Aeon's End is a cooperative deck-building game with an innovative twist: players never shuffle their decks, adding a layer of strategic planning.
The original Trivial Pursuit game featured a 'Baby Boomer' edition as one of its first expansions.
In Clue, the character Mrs. White was replaced by Dr. Orchid in 2016, the first change to the core cast in decades.
The board game app market has grown significantly, with digital versions of games like Catan, Ticket to Ride, and Pandemic available on mobile devices.
Castles of Burgundy, designed by Stefan Feld, is considered one of the best 'point salad' games where multiple strategies can lead to victory.
The original Magic: The Gathering Alpha set was printed with only 2.6 million cards in August 1993.
Imhotep, a game about building Egyptian monuments, was nominated for the Spiel des Jahres in 2016.
The world's largest Rubik's Cube is a 2.022-meter cube built as a public art installation.
Chinatown, a negotiation-heavy game set in 1960s New York City, is praised for its pure deal-making gameplay.
The Dice Tower, founded by Tom Vasel, is one of the most influential board game review websites and YouTube channels.
Concept is a party game where players use icons on a board to give clues about a word or phrase, requiring creative communication without speaking.
The earliest known depiction of people playing a board game comes from a fresco in the tomb of Egyptian pharaoh Merknera from around 3100 BCE.
Undaunted: Normandy uses deck-building mechanics within a World War II tactical combat setting.
The largest collection of board games belongs to a man in the Netherlands who owns over 15,000 different games.
Sherlock Holmes Consulting Detective requires no game board — players use a map of London, a case book, and a newspaper to solve mysteries.
Res Arcana, designed by Tom Lehmann, is a card-based engine-building game about mages competing to control magical monuments.
The phrase 'roll and move' describes one of the oldest and simplest board game mechanics, where a dice roll determines how far a player moves.
Cryptid is a deduction game where each player has a single clue about a creature's location, and they must ask questions to figure out where it is.
Monopoly's mascot, Rich Uncle Pennybags (originally known as Mr. Monopoly), was introduced in 1936 and has appeared in virtually every edition since.
The Keyflower board game combines worker placement, tile placement, and auction mechanics in a game about building a village over four seasons.
Heroquest, the classic dungeon-crawling board game, was successfully relaunched through a Hasbro Pulse crowdfunding campaign in 2020.
Hive is a two-player abstract strategy game with no board — pieces are placed to form a shifting shape, and each insect type moves differently.
The letter distribution in Scrabble was determined by Alfred Butts, who analyzed the front pages of The New York Times to calculate letter frequency.
The 2019 documentary 'Word Freak' explores the intense world of competitive Scrabble players.
Parks is a visually stunning board game about hiking through US National Parks, featuring art from the Fifty-Nine Parks print series.
The 'hobby game' market — including board games, card games, and RPGs — represents one of the fastest-growing segments in the toy industry.
A full game of Diplomacy, a classic negotiation and strategy game set in pre-World War I Europe, can take 6 to 12 hours to complete.
Betrayal Legacy applies the legacy format to the Betrayal at House on the Hill system, spanning decades of a haunted house's history.
The most common house rule in Monopoly — placing money on Free Parking — is not in the official rules and actually extends game length.
Five Tribes, designed by Bruno Cathala, uses a mancala-like mechanism where players pick up and redistribute worker meeples across a modular board.
The original Battleship game was a pad-and-pencil game before it was turned into the classic plastic version with pegs.
Matt Leacock, designer of Pandemic, was previously a user experience designer at companies like Yahoo and AOL.
Eclipse is a space exploration and civilization-building board game that combines elements of 4X strategy with Euro-style mechanics.
The number of possible chess games is estimated to be around 10^120, known as the Shannon number.
Villainous, by Ravensburger, lets players take on the roles of Disney villains, each with unique objectives and abilities.
Twilight Struggle is a two-player card-driven strategy game that simulates the Cold War and held the #1 spot on BoardGameGeek for several years.
The card game phase 10, similar to rummy, was created by Kenneth Johnson in 1982 and is sold by Mattel.
Gloomhaven's box weighs nearly 22 pounds and contains over 1,500 individual components, making it one of the heaviest mainstream board games.
Klask, a magnetic dexterity game from Denmark, has become a competitive sport with its own World Championship.
The 2023 Open Gaming License controversy caused a major community backlash against changes proposed by Wizards of the Coast for D&D.
Blank white dice have been used across nearly every culture and continent, with cubic dice being the most common shape for thousands of years.
In Carcassonne, players build a medieval landscape by placing tiles and claiming features like roads, cities, and monasteries with their meeples.
The Castles of Mad King Ludwig challenges players to build elaborate castles using rooms of different shapes and sizes.
The first Essen Spiel game fair was held in 1983 and has since grown to become the premiere event for new board game releases.
Acquire, designed by Sid Sackson in 1964, is a classic game about hotel mergers and acquisitions that is still highly regarded today.
The card game SET has been used in mathematics education to teach concepts of combinatorics and pattern recognition.
Wingspan has over 170 historically accurate bird cards, each with unique artwork and factual information about the species.
Point Salad, by Flatout Games, was named after the board game term for games offering many different ways to score points.
The longest recorded game of Risk took 12 hours and involved 5 players battling across the globe.
Lanterns: The Harvest Festival is a tile-placement game about decorating a lake with floating lanterns.
The Rubik's Cube has an official notation system using letters (U, D, L, R, F, B) to describe moves for algorithms.
Unlock! is a series of escape room card games that use a companion app for hints and puzzle verification.
Tzolk'in: The Mayan Calendar features interlocking gears on the game board that rotate each turn, changing available actions for worker placement.
The annual Board Game Awards at Gen Con include categories like Best Family Game, Best Strategy Game, and Best Party Game.
Elizabeth Magie's patent for The Landlord's Game was filed on March 23, 1903, several decades before Monopoly became a commercial product.
The word 'board' in board games comes from Old English 'bord,' meaning plank or flat surface.
In the game of bridge, a 'grand slam' means winning all 13 tricks in a hand.
The Crew: The Quest for Planet Nine is a cooperative trick-taking card game that won the Kennerspiel des Jahres in 2020.
The original version of Sorry! was invented in England in 1929 and was based on the ancient cross-and-circle game Pachisi.
Snakes and Ladders was brought to England by the British colonial administration and soon became a staple children's game worldwide.
Ark Nova, a game about building and managing a modern zoo, quickly became one of the highest-rated games on BoardGameGeek after its 2021 release.
The Potion Explosion board game uses a marble dispenser as its central mechanism, creating a physical chain-reaction puzzle.
My City, designed by Reiner Knizia, is a legacy game where each player develops their own city over 24 episodes across 8 chapters.
A 'nat 20' in Dungeons & Dragons, rolling a natural 20 on a d20, is a critical hit and one of the most celebrated moments in tabletop RPGs.
The first commercially successful crossword puzzle app for smartphones helped introduce millions of new solvers to the puzzle format.
Nemesis is a semi-cooperative sci-fi survival board game heavily inspired by the Alien film franchise, featuring detailed alien miniatures.
The card game Set was invented by population geneticist Marsha Jean Falco in 1974 while studying epilepsy in German Shepherds.
Dune: Imperium combines deck-building with worker placement in the universe of Frank Herbert's science fiction novel.
The Monopoly World Championship has been held since 1973, with contestants from dozens of countries.
El Grande, a classic area-control game set in medieval Spain, won the Spiel des Jahres in 1996 and remains highly regarded.
Tabletop Simulator, a digital sandbox for board games, allows players to import and play virtually any board game online.
The Japanese puzzle game Nonograms (also called Picross or paint-by-numbers) creates pictures by filling in grid squares according to number clues.
Pandemic's cooperative gameplay proved prophetic during the COVID-19 outbreak, with sales spiking as people connected with its theme.
The Mansions of Madness second edition replaced a human game master with an app, allowing fully cooperative play.
Letters from Whitechapel is a deduction game where one player takes the role of Jack the Ripper and others play as investigators.
The game of Othello was trademarked in 1971 by Goro Hasegawa, though similar games have existed since the 19th century.
Concordia Venus adds team play to the original Concordia game, allowing partners to coordinate strategies across the Roman Empire.
The Rubik's Cube has inspired an entire category of 'twisty puzzles' including Pyraminx, Megaminx, and Skewb.
Obsession is a Victorian-themed board game about managing an estate and climbing the social ladder through hosting events.
The longest Scrabble game ever played lasted 153 hours and was completed in 2006.
Colt Express has players program actions for bandits robbing a 3D cardboard train in the Wild West, and it won the Spiel des Jahres in 2015.
Azul: Summer Pavilion and Azul: Stained Glass of Sintra are standalone sequels that expand on the tile-drafting mechanics of the original Azul.
Dungeons & Dragons had an estimated 50 million players worldwide by 2024, its highest player count in the game's 50-year history.
The 1000-piece jigsaw puzzle is the most popular size for adult puzzlers, striking a balance between challenge and time investment.
A Feast for Odin, designed by Uwe Rosenberg, is a Viking-themed worker-placement game with over 60 different action spaces on the board.
Flamme Rouge is a cycling racing game that simulates the tactics of professional road cycling, including drafting and exhaustion.
The abstract strategy game Hive Pocket can be played anywhere because it has no board, just hexagonal tiles placed on any flat surface.
Paleo is a cooperative game set in the Stone Age that won the Kennerspiel des Jahres in 2021.
D&D co-creator Gary Gygax passed away on March 4, 2008, and tabletop gamers worldwide now celebrate 'GM's Day' on that date.
The card game Dominion has spawned over 15 expansion sets, each introducing new card types and mechanics to the deck-building formula.
Lost Ruins of Arnak combines deck-building and worker placement in an Indiana Jones-style exploration adventure.
The board game hobby has its own vocabulary, including terms like 'AP' (analysis paralysis), 'ameritrash,' 'euro,' and 'gateway game.'
Pan Am, a board game about running an airline in the mid-20th century, uses bidding and route-claiming mechanics.
The Mensa Select award recognizes five new board and card games each year that members of Mensa find particularly engaging.
Secret Hitler is a social deduction game where players are secretly assigned Liberal or Fascist roles, published in 2016 after a major Kickstarter campaign.
Mysterium Park is a streamlined version of Mysterium, designed to be played in about 30 minutes rather than the original's hour-long sessions.
In competitive Go, professional matches can last several hours, with each player sometimes receiving up to 8 hours on their clock.
The Rubik's Cube 'God's Number' of 20 was proven in 2010 by a team using 35 CPU-years of computation donated by Google.
Quacks of Quedlinburg uses a bag-building mechanic where players draw ingredient chips to brew potions, risking explosion if they push too far.
Undaunted: North Africa expanded the Undaunted system to the desert campaign theater with new mechanics for vehicles.
The board game rental subscription service model has emerged in several countries, allowing players to try games before buying.
